Relevance: 0.630 (must_read)

The Wutai Complex in the Trans-North China Orogen hosts several significant Precambrian mesothermal, greenstone-hosted gold deposits. However, as in many Precambrian orogenic belts worldwide, these deposits are associated with complex hydrothermal systems involving multistage fluid activity during successive mineralization stages. As a result, their age, origin, evolutionary history, and genetic mechanisms remain poorly constrained. Here, we present in-situ hydrothermal monazite U-Th-Pb geochronological data, together with trace-element and S isotope analyses of multiple generations of pyrite and arsenopyrite, from the representative greenstone-hosted Dongyaozhuang gold deposit in the Wutai Complex. These integrated datasets provide new constraints on the hydrothermal evolution and genesis of the deposit. Gold mineralization occurred in three distinct stages and is represented by veinlet-hosted and disseminated ores: Stage I disseminated pyrite-arsenopyrite assemblages, Stage II quartz-carbonate-pyrite veinlets, and Stage III quartz-chalcopyrite-pyrite veinlets. Six generations of pyrite were identified, including Py1 from Stage I, Py2a, Py2b, and Py2c from Stage II, and Py3a and Py3b from Stage III. Hydrothermal monazite from Stage II gold-mineralized veinlets yielded a mean 232Th/208Pb age of 1846.7 ± 4.7 Ma, which is interpreted as the timing of gold mineralization. This age is inconsistent with regional granitic magmatism at 2560–2515 Ma, 2180–2120 Ma, and ∼1800 Ma, but overlaps with metamorphic and deformational processes related to the Paleoproterozoic orogeny. Most of the gold was precipitated during Stage II, when pyrite commonly developed a complex core-mantle-rim texture. Most sulfide generations show a narrow range of δ34SV-CDT values from −3.7 ‰ to 2.6 ‰, consistent with sulfur mobilization from greenstone wall rocks and its subsequent reprecipitation at the site of ore formation. In contrast, the negative δ34SV-CDT values of Py2a (−6.7 to −2.3 ‰) suggest isotopic fractionation induced by an increase in the pH of the metamorphic ore-forming fluid. These results, together with the geological evidence, indicate that the Dongyaozhuang deposit is an orogenic gold deposit genetically related to regional metamorphism and orogenic deformation associated with the Paleoproterozoic continent-arc-continent collision between the Western and Eastern Blocks.

item.title: Occurrence and petrogenesis of diamondiferous lamprophyres: Insights into mantle metasomatism and diamond formation
item.dc:creator: Francesca Innocenzi
Maxwell C. Day
Fabrizio Nestola
Davide Novella
Mattia La Fortezza
Kristina Kublik
D. Graham Pearson
Martha G. Pamato
item.description: Lamprophyres are a rare rock-type that include different lithologies, classified mineralogically (abundance of mica, amphibole, feldspathoids) rather than geochemically (enrichment in alkalis and volatiles). Despite the attention that these rocks have attracted, due to their possible economic interest, their sources, petrogenesis and classification remain a matter of debate. One of the most intriguing aspects is the relationship of some lamprophyre sub-types with diamonds, especially because diamondiferous lamprophyres are the rarest diamond-bearing rock-types. The reason for this association and their potential genetic relationship remain poorly understood. Previous studies on lamprophyres (diamond-bearing and barren) are reviewed to improve our knowledge of peculiar mantle paragenesis, fertilization events, metasomatic agents and the relationship between lamprophyres and diamonds. Calc-alkaline lamprophyres (CALs) are often emplaced in subduction-related settings, where slab dehydration and crustal recycling can stabilize hydrous phases (amphibole/phlogopite). Low degree partial melting of shallow metasomatized sources may explain CALs petrogenesis. To reconcile this shallow formation depth with the presence of diamonds, we propose a lithospheric stacking model, that results in a highly transient geotherm during the initial phases of craton formation. Ultramatic lamprophyres (UMLs) mostly crop out along rifts and are related to a veined lithospheric and/or asthenospheric source. A direct genetic link between diamonds and UMLs can be excluded. Instead, the presence of diamondiferous UMLs relies on spatial and temporal connections (e.g., mantle domains enriched in C-H-phases). Lamprophyres are hostile magma for diamonds, due to their high fO2, H2O and CO2, along with a longer lag time and a lower volume of emplaced magma. Published: 2026-06-29T23:02:18+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.626 (consider)

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

Relevance: 0.626 (consider)

Lamprophyres are a rare rock-type that include different lithologies, classified mineralogically (abundance of mica, amphibole, feldspathoids) rather than geochemically (enrichment in alkalis and volatiles). Despite the attention that these rocks have attracted, due to their possible economic interest, their sources, petrogenesis and classification remain a matter of debate. One of the most intriguing aspects is the relationship of some lamprophyre sub-types with diamonds, especially because diamondiferous lamprophyres are the rarest diamond-bearing rock-types. The reason for this association and their potential genetic relationship remain poorly understood. Previous studies on lamprophyres (diamond-bearing and barren) are reviewed to improve our knowledge of peculiar mantle paragenesis, fertilization events, metasomatic agents and the relationship between lamprophyres and diamonds. Calc-alkaline lamprophyres (CALs) are often emplaced in subduction-related settings, where slab dehydration and crustal recycling can stabilize hydrous phases (amphibole/phlogopite). Ultramatic lamprophyres (UMLs) mostly crop out along rifts and are related to a veined lithospheric and/or asthenospheric source. A direct genetic link between diamonds and UMLs can be excluded. Instead, the presence of diamondiferous UMLs relies on spatial and temporal connections (e.g., mantle domains enriched in C-H-phases). Lamprophyres are hostile magma for diamonds, due to their high fO2, H2O and CO2, along with a longer lag time and a lower volume of emplaced magma.

item.title: Multiple Au enrichment processes in the Early Cretaceous Heilongtan–Xiejiagou deposit, Tongbai Orogen, Central China
item.dc:creator: Jun-Jie Zhou
Zhan-Ke Li
Mei-Zhen Yang
Da Ke
Shao-Rui Zhao
Ke-Qing Zong
Zaicong Wang
Bo Zu
Le-Le Ding
item.description: The Tongbai Orogen is an important Au province in central China, but the processes responsible for localized high-grade Au enrichment in its southern segment remain poorly constrained. Here, we investigate the Heilongtan–Xiejiagou Au deposit using ore geology, hand-specimen-scale element mapping, ore-stage geochronology, and in-situ trace-element and S–Pb isotope analyses of sulfides. The deposit records three stages of mineralization: Stage I quartz–sericite–pyrite alteration, Stage II quartz–pyrite vein, and Stage III quartz–polymetallic sulfide vein. Ore-stage geochronology based on Rb-Sr and U-Pb isotopic systems of Stage I sericite and monazite, and Stage III muscovite constrains the main Au–Ag mineralization to ca. 140–134 Ma. The S–Pb isotope data, together with the Early Cretaceous ages and spatial association with granitoid magmatism, indicate a major magmatic–hydrothermal contribution, with variable input from Neoproterozoic host rocks. Pyrite records a systematic evolution from As-rich invisible-Au-bearing Py-I, through porous and inclusion-rich Py-II associated with Au redistribution and polymetallic enrichment, to trace-element-poor Py-III associated with late polymetallic sulfides. These features indicate that Stage II was the main stage of Au remobilization and local reconcentration. During this stage, coupled dissolution–reprecipitation of earlier auriferous pyrite, together with additional Au input from the infiltrating fluid, generated porous reaction domains that promoted Au release, redistribution, and electrum precipitation. These results link the deposit to Early Cretaceous magmatic–hydrothermal activity and suggest that exploration in the southern Tongbai Orogen should focus on reactivated vein systems capable of localized high-grade upgrading. Overall, this study provides new insight into fluid-mediated remobilization and local reconcentration of pyrite-hosted Au in high-grade lode gold systems. Published: 2026-07-03T23:08:06+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.620 (consider)

The Tongbai Orogen is an important Au province in central China, but the processes responsible for localized high-grade Au enrichment in its southern segment remain poorly constrained. Here, we investigate the Heilongtan–Xiejiagou Au deposit using ore geology, hand-specimen-scale element mapping, ore-stage geochronology, and in-situ trace-element and S–Pb isotope analyses of sulfides. The deposit records three stages of mineralization: Stage I quartz–sericite–pyrite alteration, Stage II quartz–pyrite vein, and Stage III quartz–polymetallic sulfide vein. These results link the deposit to Early Cretaceous magmatic–hydrothermal activity and suggest that exploration in the southern Tongbai Orogen should focus on reactivated vein systems capable of localized high-grade upgrading. Overall, this study provides new insight into fluid-mediated remobilization and local reconcentration of pyrite-hosted Au in high-grade lode gold systems.

item.title: Birth, evolution, and consumption of the Neoproterozoic Adamastor Ocean: New data from the Ribeirão da Folha Ophiolite, Araçuaí Orogen
item.dc:creator: Leandro Amaral
Fabrício de Andrade Caxito
Antonio Carlos Pedrosa-Soares
Elton Dantas
Ramon Aranda
Henrique Joncew
item.description: The geological history and role of the Ribeirão da Folha Ophiolite in the construction of the Araçuaí Orogen were investigated to establish temporal and geochemical constraints for the evolution of the Adamastor Ocean. Through petrological, geochemical, and isotopic (U–Pb, Lu–Hf, and Sm–Nd) analyses, this study presents the newly discovered Granjas do Norte meta-ultramafic body, which serves as a fundamental record of the oceanic lithosphere history of the Macaúbas Basin. New U–Pb zircon ages obtained from a chloritite sample reveal a complete ca. 140 Myr metasomatic and metamorphic chronology for the northern Adamastor Ocean: from the metasomatic record of its opening at 745 ± 11 Ma to the fluid-mediated onset of subduction at 661 ± 3 Ma, culminating in regional metamorphism at 606 ± 3 Ma during advanced subduction-accretion. The juxtaposition of rocks with magmatism from intraplate (OIB) and supra-subduction zone (SSZ) settings in a tectonic mélange with a pervasively deformed mylonitic matrix, combined with the polyphasic evolution of fluids from the subducted plate and the crust, provides robust evidence that subduction was the dominant mechanism for the closure of the Adamastor Ocean. These results provide direct evidence that challenges intracontinental models and contributes to understanding the complete life history of the Adamastor Ocean. Published: 2026-06-29T15:40:35+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.619 (consider)

The geological history and role of the Ribeirão da Folha Ophiolite in the construction of the Araçuaí Orogen were investigated to establish temporal and geochemical constraints for the evolution of the Adamastor Ocean. Through petrological, geochemical, and isotopic (U–Pb, Lu–Hf, and Sm–Nd) analyses, this study presents the newly discovered Granjas do Norte meta-ultramafic body, which serves as a fundamental record of the oceanic lithosphere history of the Macaúbas Basin. The juxtaposition of rocks with magmatism from intraplate (OIB) and supra-subduction zone (SSZ) settings in a tectonic mélange with a pervasively deformed mylonitic matrix, combined with the polyphasic evolution of fluids from the subducted plate and the crust, provides robust evidence that subduction was the dominant mechanism for the closure of the Adamastor Ocean. These results provide direct evidence that challenges intracontinental models and contributes to understanding the complete life history of the Adamastor Ocean.

item.title: The Preservation of Intragranular and Intergranular Coesite in Metasedimentary Rocks: Insights From a TEM‐Based Structure Analysis
item.dc:creator: Jinyu Zheng
Tao Chen
Junfeng Zhang
Penglei Liu
Wenlong Liu
Feng Shi
Mingxing Gong
Zhe Gong
item.description: ABSTRACT Coesite is a key indicator mineral of ultrahigh‐pressure (UHP) metamorphism. A “dry” environment is widely recognized as a prerequisite for coesite preservation during exhumation. However, the deformation behaviour of coesite, its retrograde replacement by quartz and their host and surrounding minerals remain poorly constrained at the micro‐ to nanoscale, especially for coesite included in or surrounded by nonrigid minerals. Here, we employed electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD), focused ion beam (FIB) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to characterize the micro‐nanostructure of intragranular coesite (within dolomite) and intergranular coesite (surrounded by dolomite and apatite) in a UHP metasedimentary rock from the Ganjialing area, Dabie Shan region in east‐central China. Our observations reveal that both intragranular and intergranular coesite grains are essentially undeformed and dislocation‐free, whereas the palisade quartz rim enveloping coesite exhibits abundant dislocations and tangles. Based on the dislocation densities within these quartz rims, the differential stress is estimated at 347–663 MPa for intragranular coesite and 320–741 MPa for intergranular coesite grains, respectively. Combining this differential stress estimate with the regional P–T–t path constrains the corresponding metamorphic conditions to 715°C–720°C and 1.7–2.0 GPa. Furthermore, dolomite and apatite domains adjacent to coesite show higher dislocation densities than those distal to coesite. Collectively, the palisade quartz rim, dolomite and apatite form a composite buffer zone that not only maintains a closed, dry system but also accommodates the high differential stress generated by phase transformation. This buffer system effectively suppresses the replacement of coesite by quartz during decompression. Our findings demonstrate that transformation‐induced differential stress plays a pivotal role in the preservation of coesite within the mechanically weak, nonrigid matrix of UHP metasedimentary rocks during exhumation. Published: 2026-07-02T12:45:56+00:00 Venue: Journal of Metamorphic Geology Score: 0.617 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jinyu Zheng; Tao Chen; Junfeng Zhang; Penglei Liu; Wenlong Liu; Feng Shi; Mingxing Gong; Zhe Gong

Journal: Journal of Metamorphic Geology

DOI: 10.1111/jmg.70046

Relevance: 0.617 (consider)

ABSTRACT Coesite is a key indicator mineral of ultrahigh‐pressure (UHP) metamorphism. A “dry” environment is widely recognized as a prerequisite for coesite preservation during exhumation. However, the deformation behaviour of coesite, its retrograde replacement by quartz and their host and surrounding minerals remain poorly constrained at the micro‐ to nanoscale, especially for coesite included in or surrounded by nonrigid minerals. Here, we employed electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD), focused ion beam (FIB) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to characterize the micro‐nanostructure of intragranular coesite (within dolomite) and intergranular coesite (surrounded by dolomite and apatite) in a UHP metasedimentary rock from the Ganjialing area, Dabie Shan region in east‐central China. Our observations reveal that both intragranular and intergranular coesite grains are essentially undeformed and dislocation‐free, whereas the palisade quartz rim enveloping coesite exhibits abundant dislocations and tangles. Based on the dislocation densities within these quartz rims, the differential stress is estimated at 347–663 MPa for intragranular coesite and 320–741 MPa for intergranular coesite grains, respectively. Combining this differential stress estimate with the regional P–T–t path constrains the corresponding metamorphic conditions to 715°C–720°C and 1.7–2.0 GPa. Furthermore, dolomite and apatite domains adjacent to coesite show higher dislocation densities than those distal to coesite. Collectively, the palisade quartz rim, dolomite and apatite form a composite buffer zone that not only maintains a closed, dry system but also accommodates the high differential stress generated by phase transformation. This buffer system effectively suppresses the replacement of coesite by quartz during decompression. Our findings demonstrate that transformation‐induced differential stress plays a pivotal role in the preservation of coesite within the mechanically weak, nonrigid matrix of UHP metasedimentary rocks during exhumation.

item.title: Niobium and heavy rare earth element mineralization in the Bayan Obo deposit, China: insights from aeschynite mineralogy, geochemistry, and geochronology
item.dc:creator: Qing Sun
Xian-Hua Li
item.description: The Bayan Obo deposit in China is the largest light rare earth element (LREE) deposit worldwide, and also hosts substantial Nb and heavy REE (HREE) resources. However, the timing and enrichment mechanisms of the Nb and HREE mineralization are poorly constrained. Aeschynite is the most important Nb- and HREE-bearing mineral at Bayan Obo and was investigated in this study to reconstruct the history of Nb and HREE mineralization. In situ Lu–Hf isochron dating of two types of aeschynite yielded consistent ages of 435–420 Ma, which overlap with the Th–Pb isotopic ages of intergrown monazite (438–392 Ma). These results demonstrate that early Paleozoic is a significant episode for Nb and HREE mineralization. The aeschynite has a close spatiotemporal relationship with alkali silicates, suggesting that hydrothermal fluids were naturally rich in alkalis and dominantly exsolved from coeval carbonatitic magmas. Strontium isotopic compositions of aeschynite are relatively enriched and exhibit an increased trend from the type-1 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7037–0.7064) to type-2 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7072–0.7128), which was likely caused by heterogeneous crustal contamination during the evolution of hydrothermal fluids. Paragenetic aeschynite, calcite, fluorite and bastnäsite reflect that carbonate (CO3 2–) and fluoride (F-) anions played a key role for Nb mobilization. The unloading of Nb in aeschynite was usually related to the variation of Ti content in hydrothermal fluids. According to apparent correlation of REE, Th and Ca in aeschynite and developed ilmenite, Nb unloading was influenced by the processes of coupled substitution or ilmenite crystallization. The enrichment of HREE was ascribed to preferential LREE fractionation following gradual temperature decrease of CO3 2–-bearing alkaline hydrothermal fluids. Moreover, earlier crystallized LREE minerals is potential trigger for local elevation of HREE content. Published: 2026-07-04T22:23:19+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.615 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qing Sun; Xian-Hua Li

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

DOI: 10.1016/j.gsf.2026.102405

Relevance: 0.615 (consider)

The Bayan Obo deposit in China is the largest light rare earth element (LREE) deposit worldwide, and also hosts substantial Nb and heavy REE (HREE) resources. However, the timing and enrichment mechanisms of the Nb and HREE mineralization are poorly constrained. Aeschynite is the most important Nb- and HREE-bearing mineral at Bayan Obo and was investigated in this study to reconstruct the history of Nb and HREE mineralization. In situ Lu–Hf isochron dating of two types of aeschynite yielded consistent ages of 435–420 Ma, which overlap with the Th–Pb isotopic ages of intergrown monazite (438–392 Ma). These results demonstrate that early Paleozoic is a significant episode for Nb and HREE mineralization. The aeschynite has a close spatiotemporal relationship with alkali silicates, suggesting that hydrothermal fluids were naturally rich in alkalis and dominantly exsolved from coeval carbonatitic magmas. Strontium isotopic compositions of aeschynite are relatively enriched and exhibit an increased trend from the type-1 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7037–0.7064) to type-2 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7072–0.7128), which was likely caused by heterogeneous crustal contamination during the evolution of hydrothermal fluids. Paragenetic aeschynite, calcite, fluorite and bastnäsite reflect that carbonate (CO3 2–) and fluoride (F-) anions played a key role for Nb mobilization. The unloading of Nb in aeschynite was usually related to the variation of Ti content in hydrothermal fluids. According to apparent correlation of REE, Th and Ca in aeschynite and developed ilmenite, Nb unloading was influenced by the processes of coupled substitution or ilmenite crystallization. The enrichment of HREE was ascribed to preferential LREE fractionation following gradual temperature decrease of CO3 2–-bearing alkaline hydrothermal fluids. Moreover, earlier crystallized LREE minerals is potential trigger for local elevation of HREE content.

item.title: Mélange melting generates metasomatic precursors for post-collisional potassic magmas
item.dc:creator: Shidong Guan
Mingdi Gao
Yu Wang
item.description: The significant role of mélanges in the sources of various mantle-derived magmas, particularly those recording crust-mantle interactions, has been increasingly debated. However, previous experimental studies have predominantly focused on their contribution to arc magmatism, leaving the links between mélange melting and postcollisional orogenic potassic magmatism poorly constrained. Here, we conducted high-temperature and high-pressure experiments simulating the partial melting of mélange rocks—consisting of continental crustal sediment, terrigenous blueschist, and depleted peridotite—at relatively shallow mantle conditions (e.g., at 1.5–2.0 GPa and 750–1150 °C). Our results demonstrate that the mass ratios of different lithologies within the mélange critically govern the major element compositions of the resulting melts, effectively distinguishing the petrogenetic pathways of postcollisional K-rich magmas from typical primitive arc magmas. Furthermore, rather than directly representing the final erupted magmas, our experimental melts act as pristine metasomatic agents. They represent the primary stage of crust-mantle interaction during shallow imbrication, where they ascend to refertilize the overlying lithospheric mantle. The subsequent partial melting of this newly enriched mantle—driven by postcollisional lithospheric dynamics—ultimately generates the final erupted K-rich magmas with their distinct geochemical signatures. Published: 2026-07-01T23:30:45+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.612 (consider)

Authors: Shidong Guan; Mingdi Gao; Yu Wang

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108687

Relevance: 0.612 (consider)

The significant role of mélanges in the sources of various mantle-derived magmas, particularly those recording crust-mantle interactions, has been increasingly debated. However, previous experimental studies have predominantly focused on their contribution to arc magmatism, leaving the links between mélange melting and postcollisional orogenic potassic magmatism poorly constrained. Here, we conducted high-temperature and high-pressure experiments simulating the partial melting of mélange rocks—consisting of continental crustal sediment, terrigenous blueschist, and depleted peridotite—at relatively shallow mantle conditions (e.g., at 1.5–2.0 GPa and 750–1150 °C). Our results demonstrate that the mass ratios of different lithologies within the mélange critically govern the major element compositions of the resulting melts, effectively distinguishing the petrogenetic pathways of postcollisional K-rich magmas from typical primitive arc magmas. Furthermore, rather than directly representing the final erupted magmas, our experimental melts act as pristine metasomatic agents. They represent the primary stage of crust-mantle interaction during shallow imbrication, where they ascend to refertilize the overlying lithospheric mantle. The subsequent partial melting of this newly enriched mantle—driven by postcollisional lithospheric dynamics—ultimately generates the final erupted K-rich magmas with their distinct geochemical signatures.

item.title: Metallogenic potential of komatiites from the Shankaraghatta Complex, Western Dharwar Craton (Southern India)
item.dc:creator: Ria Mukherjee
Marco Fiorentini
Laure Martin
Robert Frei
Kim Baublys
Louise Schoneveld
item.description: The Shankaraghatta Complex in the Dharwar Craton (Southern India) hosts highly serpentinized komatiites containing minor disseminated nickel-sulfides as blebs of millerite–pyrite–violarite ± chalcopyrite, locally enriched in a range of semi and precious metals. This study shows that these rocks, yielding a Sm-Nd age of 2718 ± 107 Ma, are broadly coeval with the highly nickel-sulfide mineralized komatiites of the Yilgarn Craton (Western Australia), but significant mineralization is absent in the Shankaraghatta Complex and across the Dharwar Craton. This discrepancy is addressed through systematic multiple sulfur isotope characterization of the sulfides, which reveals a predominant mantle-like signature that rule out significant crustal assimilation during komatiite emplacement in the complex. It is proposed that sulfide saturation was unlikely achieved through dynamic processes typical of economically mineralized komatiite systems, such as, turbulent magma flow and interaction with sulfur-rich crustal reservoirs. Instead, sulfide formation is attributed to fractionation within a cooling magmatic system, with subsequent serpentinization-driven remobilization locally enhancing semi and precious metal concentrations, as supported by detailed sulfide trace-element geochemistry, and study of platinum-group minerals and their textures. In addition, review of regional radiogenic isotope data suggests the absence of significant lithospheric structures capable of channelizing and focussing magma flow during emplacement, indicating a stable, partially cratonized crust that hindered widespread komatiite emplacement in the Dharwar Craton. This study therefore suggests that although Dharwar komatiites may be unfavourable for nickel-sulfide mineralization, widespread serpentinization may have remobilized and locally concentrated a range of metals, leading to previously unknown styles of non-conventional mineralization. Published: 2026-06-30T02:07:54+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.610 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ria Mukherjee; Marco Fiorentini; Laure Martin; Robert Frei; Kim Baublys; Louise Schoneveld

Journal: Mineralium Deposita

DOI: 10.1007/s00126-026-01469-w

Relevance: 0.610 (consider)

The Shankaraghatta Complex in the Dharwar Craton (Southern India) hosts highly serpentinized komatiites containing minor disseminated nickel-sulfides as blebs of millerite–pyrite–violarite ± chalcopyrite, locally enriched in a range of semi and precious metals. This study shows that these rocks, yielding a Sm-Nd age of 2718 ± 107 Ma, are broadly coeval with the highly nickel-sulfide mineralized komatiites of the Yilgarn Craton (Western Australia), but significant mineralization is absent in the Shankaraghatta Complex and across the Dharwar Craton. This discrepancy is addressed through systematic multiple sulfur isotope characterization of the sulfides, which reveals a predominant mantle-like signature that rule out significant crustal assimilation during komatiite emplacement in the complex. It is proposed that sulfide saturation was unlikely achieved through dynamic processes typical of economically mineralized komatiite systems, such as, turbulent magma flow and interaction with sulfur-rich crustal reservoirs. Instead, sulfide formation is attributed to fractionation within a cooling magmatic system, with subsequent serpentinization-driven remobilization locally enhancing semi and precious metal concentrations, as supported by detailed sulfide trace-element geochemistry, and study of platinum-group minerals and their textures. In addition, review of regional radiogenic isotope data suggests the absence of significant lithospheric structures capable of channelizing and focussing magma flow during emplacement, indicating a stable, partially cratonized crust that hindered widespread komatiite emplacement in the Dharwar Craton. This study therefore suggests that although Dharwar komatiites may be unfavourable for nickel-sulfide mineralization, widespread serpentinization may have remobilized and locally concentrated a range of metals, leading to previously unknown styles of non-conventional mineralization.

item.title: Decoupling of surface and lithospheric sutures, a review of Early Mesozoic Sulu UHP orogenic belt, eastern China
item.dc:creator: Lingtong Meng
Wei Lin
Yang Chu
Wei Wei
Lin Wu
item.description: Suture zones are fundamental markers of ancient plate boundaries, essential for reconstructing paleogeography, plate kinematics, and the geodynamic evolution of orogenic systems. However, in many collisional belts, their identification remains challenging due to the absence of diagnostic elements such as ophiolitic mélanges and arc-related magmatism. Growing geophysical and geological evidence suggests that lithospheric sutures are commonly decoupled from surface sutures, further complicating their recognition. The Sulu orogenic belt, formed by the Early Mesozoic northward deep subduction of the South China block (SCB) beneath the North China block (NCB), provides a natural laboratory to address these issues. Despite extensive studies over the past three decades, the location of the suture zone remains highly debated, largely due to the Late Triassic post-orogenic to Late Mesozoic intracontinental reworking. Here, we adopt an alternative approach by focusing on the tectonic response of the overriding plate and its coupling with the behavior of the subducted plate to constrain the locations of surface and lithospheric sutures. We synthesized our new structural analysis, anisotropy of magnetic susceptibility, and geochronological data with previous study to reveal the deformation history of the overriding NCB and the multiphase exhumation of SCB. The NCB records NE–SW-trending contraction, manifested by Early–Middle Triassic (250–230 Ma) top-to-the-NE thrusting and Late Triassic (230–220 Ma) top-to-the-SW back-thrusting. Multiphase exhumation is attributed to Early–Middle Triassic syn-convergence southwestward extrusion, Latest Triassic post-orogenic extension, and Early Cretaceous rolling-hinge type detachments. By reviewing previous models, we argue that the boundary between the NCB and the Sulu orogenic belt corresponds to extensional detachment systems, whereas the surface suture is located north of the South Sulu massif and likely extends to the north of the Beihuaiyang belt in the Dabie orogen. The identification of SCB-sourced material exhumed within the overriding NCB reveals a two-layer crustal architecture of the southeastern NCB, indicating that a 200-km-long tract of the NCB's lithosphere was bulldozed and replaced by the flat-slab subduction of the SCB. This process drove overriding-plate contraction and decoupled the surface from the lithospheric suture. We thus locate the lithospheric suture beneath Bohai Bay, ~200 km north of the surface suture. Finally, we propose a comprehensive tectonic model involving Late Permian–Early Triassic oceanic subduction, slab break-off, followed by continental deep subduction of the SCB, Early–Middle Triassic syn-convergence exhumation accompanied with overriding-plate contraction driven by flat-slab subduction, Latest Triassic post-orogenic extension, and Early Cretaceous reworking. This study highlights the importance of linking overriding-plate deformation with subduction process to trace lithospheric and surface sutures and explain their decoupling in complex orogens. Published: 2026-06-30T23:18:57+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.610 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lingtong Meng; Wei Lin; Yang Chu; Wei Wei; Lin Wu

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105607

Relevance: 0.610 (consider)

Suture zones are fundamental markers of ancient plate boundaries, essential for reconstructing paleogeography, plate kinematics, and the geodynamic evolution of orogenic systems. However, in many collisional belts, their identification remains challenging due to the absence of diagnostic elements such as ophiolitic mélanges and arc-related magmatism. Growing geophysical and geological evidence suggests that lithospheric sutures are commonly decoupled from surface sutures, further complicating their recognition. The Sulu orogenic belt, formed by the Early Mesozoic northward deep subduction of the South China block (SCB) beneath the North China block (NCB), provides a natural laboratory to address these issues. Despite extensive studies over the past three decades, the location of the suture zone remains highly debated, largely due to the Late Triassic post-orogenic to Late Mesozoic intracontinental reworking. Here, we adopt an alternative approach by focusing on the tectonic response of the overriding plate and its coupling with the behavior of the subducted plate to constrain the locations of surface and lithospheric sutures. We synthesized our new structural analysis, anisotropy of magnetic susceptibility, and geochronological data with previous study to reveal the deformation history of the overriding NCB and the multiphase exhumation of SCB. The NCB records NE–SW-trending contraction, manifested by Early–Middle Triassic (250–230 Ma) top-to-the-NE thrusting and Late Triassic (230–220 Ma) top-to-the-SW back-thrusting. Multiphase exhumation is attributed to Early–Middle Triassic syn-convergence southwestward extrusion, Latest Triassic post-orogenic extension, and Early Cretaceous rolling-hinge type detachments. By reviewing previous models, we argue that the boundary between the NCB and the Sulu orogenic belt corresponds to extensional detachment systems, whereas the surface suture is located north of the South Sulu massif and likely extends to the north of the Beihuaiyang belt in the Dabie orogen. The identification of SCB-sourced material exhumed within the overriding NCB reveals a two-layer crustal architecture of the southeastern NCB, indicating that a 200-km-long tract of the NCB's lithosphere was bulldozed and replaced by the flat-slab subduction of the SCB. Finally, we propose a comprehensive tectonic model involving Late Permian–Early Triassic oceanic subduction, slab break-off, followed by continental deep subduction of the SCB, Early–Middle Triassic syn-convergence exhumation accompanied with overriding-plate contraction driven by flat-slab subduction, Latest Triassic post-orogenic extension, and Early Cretaceous reworking. This study highlights the importance of linking overriding-plate deformation with subduction process to trace lithospheric and surface sutures and explain their decoupling in complex orogens.

item.title: Precise age constraints for the Huangjindong gold deposit, South China
item.dc:creator: Yulong Lu
Zengxian Luo
Fei Wang
Peng Wang
Jianzhong Huang
Huan Li
Qingquan Liu
Guojun Dong
Chunhua Wen
Defeng Shi
Enbo Tu
Yang Liu
Zhen Tan
item.description: The large Huangjindong gold deposit, which was positioned in the Jiangnan Orogen, has developed through an early scheelite-gold mineralization epoch and a later wolframite mineralization epoch. The ore-forming age remains poorly constrained, ranging from 462 Ma to 129.7 Ma, mainly due to a lack of appropriate dating minerals and uncertainties in mineral paragenesis. The current work identified hydrothermal rutile paragenetic with gold-bearing arsenopyrite as well as post-mineralization wolframite. These rutile and wolframite grains were subjected to in-situ LA-ICP-MS U–Pb dating, resulting in an age of 380.6 ± 6.6 Ma for the principal ore-forming stage during the scheelite-gold epoch and 158.8 ± 6.6 Ma for the wolframite mineralization epoch. The obtained ages conform to the observed ore-forming processes, implying that the primary gold mineralization of this deposit happened in the Devonian period. Combined with recently obtained precise ages for gold deposits in South China, this work emphasizes that the Silurian–Devonian was an important period for gold mineralization. The gold mineralization event at Huangjindong is indicative of Silurian–Devonian tectono-magmatic-hydrothermal activity in South China, formed within an extensional tectonic framework associated with intracontinental orogenic evolution. This paper also highlights that in-situ dating of U-bearing accessory minerals is an effective method for precisely constraining mineralization timing in deposits characterized by multi-pulse hydrothermal activity. Published: 2026-06-29T16:05:44+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.599 (consider)

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107417

Relevance: 0.599 (consider)

The large Huangjindong gold deposit, which was positioned in the Jiangnan Orogen, has developed through an early scheelite-gold mineralization epoch and a later wolframite mineralization epoch. The ore-forming age remains poorly constrained, ranging from 462 Ma to 129.7 Ma, mainly due to a lack of appropriate dating minerals and uncertainties in mineral paragenesis. These rutile and wolframite grains were subjected to in-situ LA-ICP-MS U–Pb dating, resulting in an age of 380.6 ± 6.6 Ma for the principal ore-forming stage during the scheelite-gold epoch and 158.8 ± 6.6 Ma for the wolframite mineralization epoch. The obtained ages conform to the observed ore-forming processes, implying that the primary gold mineralization of this deposit happened in the Devonian period. Combined with recently obtained precise ages for gold deposits in South China, this work emphasizes that the Silurian–Devonian was an important period for gold mineralization. The gold mineralization event at Huangjindong is indicative of Silurian–Devonian tectono-magmatic-hydrothermal activity in South China, formed within an extensional tectonic framework associated with intracontinental orogenic evolution. This paper also highlights that in-situ dating of U-bearing accessory minerals is an effective method for precisely constraining mineralization timing in deposits characterized by multi-pulse hydrothermal activity.

item.title: Subseafloor processes in ultramafic-influenced volcanic-hosted hydrothermal systems: An insight from the Gnitahei massive sulfide mineralization, Arctic Mid-Ocean Ridges
item.dc:creator: Sabina Strmić Palinkaš
Marie Wold
Rolf Birger Pedersen
Håvard Hallås Stubseid
Fredrik Sahlström
Lahaye Yann
Leif-Erik Rydland Pedersen
Jorge E. Spangenberg
item.description: The extinct Gnitahei seafloor massive sulfide (SMS) deposit was discovered in 2019 on the ultraslow spreading Mohns Ridge (Arctic Mid-Ocean Ridge System, 72.8°N, 3.81°E, 3022–2760 mbsl). It provides a unique opportunity to study subseafloor hydrothermal processes without drilling, as tectonic uplift and ongoing mass wasting expose the deeper parts of the hydrothermal system. The deposit is hosted by hydrothermally altered basalts within the footwall of a NE–SW striking normal fault, in proximity to the active Fåvne Vent Field. Driven by extensional tectonics and facilitated by the thin oceanic crust along the ultraslow-spreading Mohns Ridge, hydrothermal circulation likely extends deep into the serpentinized upper mantle. Hydrothermal alteration exhibits vertical zonation. Chloritization accompanied by remobilization of silica, LILEs, and REEs dominate the deeper parts of the system. In contrast, silicification associated with an enrichment in LILEs and REEs characterizes the upper part of the Gnitahei SMS deposit. The paleosurface is clearly marked by a layer of red jasper. The sulfide mineralization is dominated by pyrite and marcasite, with euhedral pyrite prevailing in deeper zones and colloform pyrite being more abundant near the paleosurface. Native gold occurs mostly as inclusions in pyrite. Trace-element data obtained from pyrite, including elevated Ni and Co concentrations and a high Au/Ag ratios, are consistent with fluid interaction with underlying ultramafic rocks. Sulfur isotope composition of pyrite reveals a strong 34S enrichment (bulk δ34SVCDT = 11.1–15.9‰; in-situ δ34SVCDT = 10.8–17.3‰), indicating a significant contribution of reduced seawater sulfate, which constitutes ~50% of the total sulfur according to a two-component mixing model. The high influx of reduced sulfate is attributed to reducing conditions driven by serpentinization of ultramafic lithologies. Under these conditions, seawater sulfate can be reduced prior to anhydrite deposition, allowing isotopically heavy sulfur to infiltrate the hydrothermal system. Depth-dependent decreases in δ34S values likely reflect greater contributions of reduced seawater sulfate in shallower zones and/or enhanced fractionation at lower temperatures. Published: 2026-06-29T16:00:39+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.598 (consider)

Authors: Sabina Strmić Palinkaš; Marie Wold; Rolf Birger Pedersen; Håvard Hallås Stubseid; Fredrik Sahlström; Lahaye Yann; Leif-Erik Rydland Pedersen; Jorge E. Spangenberg

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123585

Relevance: 0.598 (consider)

The extinct Gnitahei seafloor massive sulfide (SMS) deposit was discovered in 2019 on the ultraslow spreading Mohns Ridge (Arctic Mid-Ocean Ridge System, 72.8°N, 3.81°E, 3022–2760 mbsl). It provides a unique opportunity to study subseafloor hydrothermal processes without drilling, as tectonic uplift and ongoing mass wasting expose the deeper parts of the hydrothermal system. The deposit is hosted by hydrothermally altered basalts within the footwall of a NE–SW striking normal fault, in proximity to the active Fåvne Vent Field. Driven by extensional tectonics and facilitated by the thin oceanic crust along the ultraslow-spreading Mohns Ridge, hydrothermal circulation likely extends deep into the serpentinized upper mantle. Hydrothermal alteration exhibits vertical zonation. Chloritization accompanied by remobilization of silica, LILEs, and REEs dominate the deeper parts of the system. In contrast, silicification associated with an enrichment in LILEs and REEs characterizes the upper part of the Gnitahei SMS deposit. The paleosurface is clearly marked by a layer of red jasper. The sulfide mineralization is dominated by pyrite and marcasite, with euhedral pyrite prevailing in deeper zones and colloform pyrite being more abundant near the paleosurface. Native gold occurs mostly as inclusions in pyrite. Trace-element data obtained from pyrite, including elevated Ni and Co concentrations and a high Au/Ag ratios, are consistent with fluid interaction with underlying ultramafic rocks. Sulfur isotope composition of pyrite reveals a strong 34S enrichment (bulk δ34SVCDT = 11.1–15.9‰; in-situ δ34SVCDT = 10.8–17.3‰), indicating a significant contribution of reduced seawater sulfate, which constitutes ~50% of the total sulfur according to a two-component mixing model. The high influx of reduced sulfate is attributed to reducing conditions driven by serpentinization of ultramafic lithologies. Under these conditions, seawater sulfate can be reduced prior to anhydrite deposition, allowing isotopically heavy sulfur to infiltrate the hydrothermal system. Depth-dependent decreases in δ34S values likely reflect greater contributions of reduced seawater sulfate in shallower zones and/or enhanced fractionation at lower temperatures.

item.title: Linking Himalayan uplift to crustal deformation: Structural and multi-chronometer constraints from the Kangmar dome
item.dc:creator: Zhen Li
Pengfei Li
Dewen Zheng
Fred Jourdan
Gideon Rosenbaum
Qiang Wang
item.description: The highest topography on Earth occurs in the Himalayan orogen, which is the quintessential example of continental collision. To elucidate the crustal processes that accompanied the rise of the Himalayas, we investigated the deformational history of the Kangmar dome in the eastern Himalayas. By integrating field mapping with monazite U-Th-Pb, mica 40Ar/39Ar, and zircon and apatite (UTh)/He geochronology, we identified three distinct phases of deformation. The first phase involved north-south contractional deformation at ca. 51–44 Ma. This was followed by pervasive top-to-north ductile shearing at ca. 26–17 Ma, which exhumed deep-crustal rocks. North-south shortening was responsible for the final doming, at ca. 15–8 Ma, which involved two distinct cooling and exhumation stages: an earlier bottom-up cooling event that was driven by underthrusting of the Tethyan Himalayan Sequence below the Kangmar dome, and a later pop-up exhumation of the dome core. These deformation phases, which were driven by India-Asia convergence, are correlated with structural patterns across the Himalayan orogen. The deformation record is temporally linked to the episodic uplift history of the Himalayas, thus elucidating potential driving mechanisms. Early Eocene contraction was likely responsible for elevating the Himalayas above sea level. The main rise of the Himalayas, which occurred during the Early Miocene, was possibly triggered by the southward migration of deep-crustal rocks toward the southern Himalayan front. Collectively, the deformation history of the Kangmar dome provides critical insights into the tectonic evolution of this collisional orogen, including its uplift history. Published: 2026-07-05T21:54:24+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.596 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhen Li; Pengfei Li; Dewen Zheng; Fred Jourdan; Gideon Rosenbaum; Qiang Wang

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105616

Relevance: 0.596 (consider)

The highest topography on Earth occurs in the Himalayan orogen, which is the quintessential example of continental collision. To elucidate the crustal processes that accompanied the rise of the Himalayas, we investigated the deformational history of the Kangmar dome in the eastern Himalayas. By integrating field mapping with monazite U-Th-Pb, mica 40Ar/39Ar, and zircon and apatite (UTh)/He geochronology, we identified three distinct phases of deformation. The first phase involved north-south contractional deformation at ca. 51–44 Ma. This was followed by pervasive top-to-north ductile shearing at ca. 26–17 Ma, which exhumed deep-crustal rocks. North-south shortening was responsible for the final doming, at ca. 15–8 Ma, which involved two distinct cooling and exhumation stages: an earlier bottom-up cooling event that was driven by underthrusting of the Tethyan Himalayan Sequence below the Kangmar dome, and a later pop-up exhumation of the dome core. These deformation phases, which were driven by India-Asia convergence, are correlated with structural patterns across the Himalayan orogen. The deformation record is temporally linked to the episodic uplift history of the Himalayas, thus elucidating potential driving mechanisms. Early Eocene contraction was likely responsible for elevating the Himalayas above sea level. The main rise of the Himalayas, which occurred during the Early Miocene, was possibly triggered by the southward migration of deep-crustal rocks toward the southern Himalayan front. Collectively, the deformation history of the Kangmar dome provides critical insights into the tectonic evolution of this collisional orogen, including its uplift history.

item.title: Comment on “Super-reduced and ultra-high pressure minerals in ophiolites: A critical review and the case for meteorite impact” by
item.dc:creator: Zhiyun Lu
Jingsui Yang
item.description: Su and Jing (2025) hypothesized that meteorite impacts could have formed ultra-reduced and ultra-high-pressure minerals (especially microdiamonds) within ophiolites. Although competing genetic models enrich academic debate, systematic comparison between impact diamonds (from Popigai, Kara, Ukrainian craters and ureilite meteorites) and ophiolite-hosted diamonds reveals fundamental discrepancies across five key indices: crystal morphology, internal microstructures, nitrogen impurity characteristics, carbon isotope signatures and tectonic settings. Comprehensive mineralogical, geochemical and geological observations collectively rule out an impact origin for ophiolite-hosted diamonds. Published: 2026-07-01T06:39:22+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.595 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhiyun Lu; Jingsui Yang

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105606

Relevance: 0.595 (consider)

Su and Jing (2025) hypothesized that meteorite impacts could have formed ultra-reduced and ultra-high-pressure minerals (especially microdiamonds) within ophiolites. Although competing genetic models enrich academic debate, systematic comparison between impact diamonds (from Popigai, Kara, Ukrainian craters and ureilite meteorites) and ophiolite-hosted diamonds reveals fundamental discrepancies across five key indices: crystal morphology, internal microstructures, nitrogen impurity characteristics, carbon isotope signatures and tectonic settings. Comprehensive mineralogical, geochemical and geological observations collectively rule out an impact origin for ophiolite-hosted diamonds.

item.title: Podiform chromitites as multi-stage mantle archives: Integrating deep-mantle inheritance with shallow mantle processes
item.dc:creator: Fahui Xiong
Basem Zoheir
Weibin Gui
Huidan Xie
Xiangzhen Xu
Tian Qiu
Jingsui Yang
item.description: Podiform chromitites hosted in ophiolitic mantle peridotites preserve mineralogical and geochemical signatures formed under contrasting pressure–temperature–redox conditions, making them among the most debated lithologies in Mantle Geology. Field relationships, internal structures, and chromite compositions consistently indicate formation during melt–rock interaction and focused melt transport in the shallow upper mantle. However, these rocks also contain diverse mineral inclusions, including platinum-group minerals (PGMs), ultrahigh-pressure (UHP) phases, ultra-reduced minerals, crust-derived components, hydrous silicates, and amorphous materials that are difficult to reconcile within the single-stage genetic models. This review integrates global mineralogical, petrographic, geochemical, isotopic (ReOs, SrNd), and nanoscale observations to reassess the origin and significance of podiform chromitites in the context of mantle evolution and crust–mantle recycling. To distinguish observational constraints from interpretation, the evidence is evaluated using a hierarchical confidence framework based on petrographic context, analytical verification, and reproducibility. High-confidence observations include in situ inclusions, confirmed by multiple analytical techniques, whereas ultra-reduced phases and amorphous materials are treated more cautiously because shallow or secondary origins cannot constantly be excluded. The compiled evidence indicates that podiform chromitites are evolving mineralogical systems capable of trapping and preserving materials formed under variable mantle conditions. We propose a four-stage evolutionary framework involving: (1) deep-mantle preconditioning and material recycling, (2) chromitite segregation during melt extraction in the shallow upper mantle, (3) modification by supra-subduction zone and plume-related melts and fluids, and (4) late-stage metasomatism and hydrothermal overprinting. Within this framework, structural relationships constrain chromitite formation to shallow upper-mantle melt–rock interaction systems, whereas some inclusions and microstructures are consistent with inheritance of recycled high-pressure components transported from deeper mantle domains. Podiform chromitites are therefore interpreted as time-integrated archives of mantle processes recording melt transport, redox heterogeneity, fluid–rock interaction, and long-term crust–mantle recycling without requiring chromite crystallization at mantle transition zone depths. Published: 2026-06-30T20:19:56+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.594 (consider)

Podiform chromitites hosted in ophiolitic mantle peridotites preserve mineralogical and geochemical signatures formed under contrasting pressure–temperature–redox conditions, making them among the most debated lithologies in Mantle Geology. Field relationships, internal structures, and chromite compositions consistently indicate formation during melt–rock interaction and focused melt transport in the shallow upper mantle. However, these rocks also contain diverse mineral inclusions, including platinum-group minerals (PGMs), ultrahigh-pressure (UHP) phases, ultra-reduced minerals, crust-derived components, hydrous silicates, and amorphous materials that are difficult to reconcile within the single-stage genetic models. This review integrates global mineralogical, petrographic, geochemical, isotopic (ReOs, SrNd), and nanoscale observations to reassess the origin and significance of podiform chromitites in the context of mantle evolution and crust–mantle recycling. To distinguish observational constraints from interpretation, the evidence is evaluated using a hierarchical confidence framework based on petrographic context, analytical verification, and reproducibility. High-confidence observations include in situ inclusions, confirmed by multiple analytical techniques, whereas ultra-reduced phases and amorphous materials are treated more cautiously because shallow or secondary origins cannot constantly be excluded. The compiled evidence indicates that podiform chromitites are evolving mineralogical systems capable of trapping and preserving materials formed under variable mantle conditions. We propose a four-stage evolutionary framework involving: (1) deep-mantle preconditioning and material recycling, (2) chromitite segregation during melt extraction in the shallow upper mantle, (3) modification by supra-subduction zone and plume-related melts and fluids, and (4) late-stage metasomatism and hydrothermal overprinting. Within this framework, structural relationships constrain chromitite formation to shallow upper-mantle melt–rock interaction systems, whereas some inclusions and microstructures are consistent with inheritance of recycled high-pressure components transported from deeper mantle domains. Podiform chromitites are therefore interpreted as time-integrated archives of mantle processes recording melt transport, redox heterogeneity, fluid–rock interaction, and long-term crust–mantle recycling without requiring chromite crystallization at mantle transition zone depths.

item.title: Interface Deformation, Exhumation, and Underplating: The Role of Plate Velocity Changes Throughout the Evolution of a Subduction Zone
item.dc:creator: Valeria Turino
Adam F. Holt
Derek J. Neuharth
Ryan K. Stoner
Whitney M. Behr
item.description: Abstract Exhumed metamorphic rocks yield critical insights into the mechanical, chemical, and thermal structure of the plate interface that can be used to infer exhumation pathways and their evolution throughout a subduction zone's lifetime. Metamorphosed oceanic rocks often record exhumation and underplating over relatively punctuated time periods, which appear to be linked to specific stages of subduction. Although plate velocities also vary from one stage of subduction to the next, the influence of this time dependence on exhumation and underplating remains unclear. We therefore use 2‐D time‐dependent subduction models to investigate how evolving plate velocities affect the timing and distribution of exhumation and underplating. By extracting plate velocities from a freely evolving subduction model and imposing them on kinematically driven models, we isolate the effects of realistic velocity variations on interface processes without the complexity of a dynamically evolving slab. We also explore the role of interface strength by varying its rheology and composition. In our models, exhumation consistently initiates during a punctuated detachment event following decreases in subduction velocity. Shallow exhumation ( Published: 2026-07-03T18:01:01+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.592 (consider)

We also explore the role of interface strength by varying its rheology and composition. item.title: Tracing hydrothermal fluid evolution of the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit (Eastern China) using integrated infrared spectroscopic characteristics and magnetic susceptibility
item.dc:creator: Shili Peng
Shitao Zhang
Hao Xu
Gaobin Chu
Longfei He
Yu Zhang
Hua Zhang
Rucao Li
Yihui Lyu
Di Ding
Siyuan Zhang
Haisen Huang
item.description: The Middle–Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt (MLYRB) is one of the dominant skarn–porphyry Cu–Au metallogenic belts in Eastern China. Within this belt, skarn–porphyry Cu–Au deposits show a close genetic linkage to Late Mesozoic high‑K calc‑alkaline granitoids, and are associated with variable degrees of critical metals mineralization (i.e., Co, Te, Se and Re). Previous studies have generally suggested that these granitoids possess limited W metallogenic potential; nevertheless, several concealed medium‑to‑large W–Cu–Mo deposits have been discovered in their peripheral areas in recent years. At present, the hydrothermal fluid evolution of these W‑rich skarn–porphyry Cu polymetallic deposits remains poorly constrained, and their deep exploration indicators still need further optimization. This study focuses on the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit in the westernmost segment of the MLYRB. The deposit hosts contained metal resources of 0.55 Mt Cu, 0.01 Mt Mo, and 0.012 Mt WO3, with ore grades of 0.86 wt%, 0.10 wt%, and 0.19 wt%, respectively. By combining detailed petrographic observations with short-wave infrared (SWIR) and thermal infrared (TIR) spectral alteration mapping, this study pinpoints the precise ore-forming stages and delineates detailed alteration zoning features in the Tongshankou Cu–Mo–W deposit. Building on SWIR and TIR spectral parameters, alongside volumetric bulk magnetic susceptibility (Kbulk ) measurements, we propose four mineral exploration indicators for this deposit: (i) the widespread development of kaolinite, dickite, skarn, and retrograde alteration mineral assemblages; (ii) Fe-rich chlorite, identifiable by its characteristic absorption wavelength with Pos2250 > 2250 nm; (iii) white mica–montmorillonite, distinguished by a diagnostic absorption wavelength with Pos2200 1.0 × 10-3SI). Notably, the presence of kaolinite and dickite along the intrusive contact zones between ore-forming porphyries and carbonate wall rocks provides direct evidence for acidic fluid activity during the Cu–Mo–W mineralization process. The development of acid fluid conditions in the system is tightly linked to hydrogen ion release during the precipitation of iron oxides and sulfide minerals. Collectively, these results reveal that hydrothermal fluids associated with Cu–Mo–W mineralization in the Tongshankou deposit evolved from alkaline to acidic throughout the mineralization process. Combined with previous research advances, the present findings further suggest that acidic hydrothermal fluids likely represent a ubiquitous component of skarn-hosted Cu–Mo–W mineralization systems. Clay indicator minerals, including kaolinite and dickite, provide robust constraints on the hydrothermal fluid evolution of Tongshankou deposit and serve as newly recognized, effective exploration proxies for analogous skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposits worldwide. Significantly, this study demonstrates that the integrated application of SWIR and TIR spectroscopic analyses combined with magnetic susceptibility measurements provides a rigorous and reliable approach for identifying concealed skarn–porphyry ore bodies across the MLYRB, Eastern China. Published: 2026-07-05T22:00:24+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.591 (consider)

The Middle–Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt (MLYRB) is one of the dominant skarn–porphyry Cu–Au metallogenic belts in Eastern China. Within this belt, skarn–porphyry Cu–Au deposits show a close genetic linkage to Late Mesozoic high‑K calc‑alkaline granitoids, and are associated with variable degrees of critical metals mineralization (i.e., Co, Te, Se and Re). Previous studies have generally suggested that these granitoids possess limited W metallogenic potential; nevertheless, several concealed medium‑to‑large W–Cu–Mo deposits have been discovered in their peripheral areas in recent years. At present, the hydrothermal fluid evolution of these W‑rich skarn–porphyry Cu polymetallic deposits remains poorly constrained, and their deep exploration indicators still need further optimization. This study focuses on the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit in the westernmost segment of the MLYRB. The deposit hosts contained metal resources of 0.55 Mt Cu, 0.01 Mt Mo, and 0.012 Mt WO3, with ore grades of 0.86 wt%, 0.10 wt%, and 0.19 wt%, respectively. By combining detailed petrographic observations with short-wave infrared (SWIR) and thermal infrared (TIR) spectral alteration mapping, this study pinpoints the precise ore-forming stages and delineates detailed alteration zoning features in the Tongshankou Cu–Mo–W deposit. Building on SWIR and TIR spectral parameters, alongside volumetric bulk magnetic susceptibility (Kbulk ) measurements, we propose four mineral exploration indicators for this deposit: (i) the widespread development of kaolinite, dickite, skarn, and retrograde alteration mineral assemblages; (ii) Fe-rich chlorite, identifiable by its characteristic absorption wavelength with Pos2250 > 2250 nm; (iii) white mica–montmorillonite, distinguished by a diagnostic absorption wavelength with Pos2200 1.0 × 10-3SI). Notably, the presence of kaolinite and dickite along the intrusive contact zones between ore-forming porphyries and carbonate wall rocks provides direct evidence for acidic fluid activity during the Cu–Mo–W mineralization process. Significantly, this study demonstrates that the integrated application of SWIR and TIR spectroscopic analyses combined with magnetic susceptibility measurements provides a rigorous and reliable approach for identifying concealed skarn–porphyry ore bodies across the MLYRB, Eastern China.

item.title: Formation of Cu‐Bearing and Mo‐Bearing Miarolitic Cavities During the Magmatic–Hydrothermal Transition in Porphyry Metallogenic Systems
item.dc:creator: Chu Wu
Zhaoxia Liao
Tao Hong
Zuo Ge
Xiao Zheng
Di Liu
Xingwang Xu
item.description: ABSTRACT Miarolitic cavities (MCs) serve as key indicators of volatile phase exsolution during magmatic differentiation, providing critical insights into the transition between igneous and hydrothermal regimes. Despite their metallogenic significance, the mechanisms controlling the selective extraction of metal‐bearing fluids remain poorly understood. This study investigates the microstructural and geochemical characteristics of chalcopyrite‐ and molybdenite‐dominated MCs in the Hongyuan porphyry Mo–Cu deposit (NW China). The cavities exhibit sulfide cores surrounded by graphic quartz‐feldspar, aplitic, and K‐alteration halos. Mass balance calculations reveal enrichments in Cu, Fe, Na, and Si during the magmatic–hydrothermal transition. The term “sulfide‐silicate bulb” refers to silica‐saturated fluid pockets or bubbles that develop during hypogene copper mineralization in porphyry deposits. The central sulfide‐silicate bulbs represent hypersolidus mineral assemblages, with differential Cu–Mo precipitation linked to quartz growth rates. Late‐stage meteoric water alteration further influences metal precipitation. We propose a genetic model wherein sulfide‐silicate bulbs act as the primary vehicles for the extraction, transport, and focused precipitation of Cu‐ and Mo‐rich fluids. Our results highlight the critical role of quartz growth rates and meteoric fluid influx in controlling metal segregation, providing a new framework for understanding the spatial zonation in porphyry Cu–Mo systems. Published: 2026-07-01T16:50:02+00:00 Venue: Resource Geology Score: 0.591 (consider)

ABSTRACT Miarolitic cavities (MCs) serve as key indicators of volatile phase exsolution during magmatic differentiation, providing critical insights into the transition between igneous and hydrothermal regimes. Despite their metallogenic significance, the mechanisms controlling the selective extraction of metal‐bearing fluids remain poorly understood. This study investigates the microstructural and geochemical characteristics of chalcopyrite‐ and molybdenite‐dominated MCs in the Hongyuan porphyry Mo–Cu deposit (NW China). The cavities exhibit sulfide cores surrounded by graphic quartz‐feldspar, aplitic, and K‐alteration halos. Mass balance calculations reveal enrichments in Cu, Fe, Na, and Si during the magmatic–hydrothermal transition. The term “sulfide‐silicate bulb” refers to silica‐saturated fluid pockets or bubbles that develop during hypogene copper mineralization in porphyry deposits. The central sulfide‐silicate bulbs represent hypersolidus mineral assemblages, with differential Cu–Mo precipitation linked to quartz growth rates. Late‐stage meteoric water alteration further influences metal precipitation. We propose a genetic model wherein sulfide‐silicate bulbs act as the primary vehicles for the extraction, transport, and focused precipitation of Cu‐ and Mo‐rich fluids. Our results highlight the critical role of quartz growth rates and meteoric fluid influx in controlling metal segregation, providing a new framework for understanding the spatial zonation in porphyry Cu–Mo systems.

item.title: Geochronology and genesis of rare-metal mineralization in the Himalayan leucogranite-pegmatite system
item.dc:creator: Menglan Ran
Xiaoming Sun
Ying Liu
Zhengpeng Ding
Guangming Li
item.description: Himalayan leucogranites are a key target for rare-metal exploration, yet the sources and petrogenetic evolution of their parental magmas remain debated. This study investigates three representative deposits hosted by leucogranite-pegmatite systems in distinct tectonic units of the Himalayan orogen, integrating apatite and cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock and apatite geochemistry, and apatite oxygen isotopes. Combined cassiterite and apatite dating reveals that rare-metal mineralization developed through multiple discrete magmatic-hydrothermal pulses rather than a single event, defining a prolonged, multi-stage metallogenic framework. Whole-rock and apatite trace-element signatures indicate highly fractionated, relatively oxidized melts that experienced extensive fluid exsolution, consistent with derivation from ancient metasedimentary sources within the Higher Himalayan Crystalline Sequence. These pegmatites likely evolved from parental leucogranitic magmas through extreme fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution. Apatite oxygen isotopes further resolve three contrasting fluid-evolution paths among the deposits. These range from open-system overprinting by external fluids, through continuous internal magmatic-to-hydrothermal evolution, to a largely closed system dominated by extreme fractionation, with each path corresponding to a distinct mineralization style. Collectively, these data suggest that post-collisional crustal decompression promoted partial melting of metasedimentary rocks, and the resultant leucogranitic magmas underwent high-degree fractionation and fluid exsolution to form rare-metal-bearing pegmatites. These findings constrain crustal melting, magmatic differentiation, and rare-metal enrichment in the Himalayan collisional orogen and highlight apatite as a sensitive tracer of pegmatite-forming processes. Published: 2026-07-03T06:44:11+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.588 (consider)

This study investigates three representative deposits hosted by leucogranite-pegmatite systems in distinct tectonic units of the Himalayan orogen, integrating apatite and cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock and apatite geochemistry, and apatite oxygen isotopes. Whole-rock and apatite trace-element signatures indicate highly fractionated, relatively oxidized melts that experienced extensive fluid exsolution, consistent with derivation from ancient metasedimentary sources within the Higher Himalayan Crystalline Sequence. These pegmatites likely evolved from parental leucogranitic magmas through extreme fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution. Apatite oxygen isotopes further resolve three contrasting fluid-evolution paths among the deposits. These range from open-system overprinting by external fluids, through continuous internal magmatic-to-hydrothermal evolution, to a largely closed system dominated by extreme fractionation, with each path corresponding to a distinct mineralization style. Collectively, these data suggest that post-collisional crustal decompression promoted partial melting of metasedimentary rocks, and the resultant leucogranitic magmas underwent high-degree fractionation and fluid exsolution to form rare-metal-bearing pegmatites. These findings constrain crustal melting, magmatic differentiation, and rare-metal enrichment in the Himalayan collisional orogen and highlight apatite as a sensitive tracer of pegmatite-forming processes.

item.title: Fingerprints of s-cl-bearing fluids deduced from metal and mineral microinclusions in Quaternary Kamchatka lavas
item.dc:creator: Pavel Kepezhinskas
Nikolai Berdnikov
Nadezhda Potapova
Valeria Krutikova
item.description: Quaternary ankaramite, basalt, andesite and dacite from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes in Kamchatka contain numerous mineral microinclusions in rock-forming silicate and accessory oxide minerals as well as in groundmass volcanic glass. We have studied the composition and textural setting of these microinclusions along with the petrology and geochemictsry of their host lavas using optical petrography, major and trace element geochemistry and scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS). The microinclusions are represented by Au and Au-bearing alloys, Ag and Ag-bearing alloys, partially oxidized siderophile (W) and chalcophile (Pb, Zn, Cu, Bi, Sb, Pb) metals. S-Cl-bearing microinclusions as Ag (± Cu) chlorides, composite S-O-C-bearing metal chlorides, Ag (±Cu) sulfides, sulfides of Fe, Ni, Cu, Pb, Zn and Sb as well as barite, cassiterite and apatite are very characteristic for these rocks. Primary-textured, magmatic microinclusions in olivine, pyroxenes, amphibole, plagioclase and fresh volcanic glass show no evidence for the later-stage transformations. Secondary-textured, post-magmatic microinclusions are localized in pores, voids and fractures or along the boundaries of silicate minerals and in association with secondary minerals. Magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing microinclusion assemblages almost completely overlap compositionally suggesting that S-Cl-bearing fluids accompanied formation of Kamchatka lavas from magmatic differentiation in sub-volcanic conduits to their emplacement, cooling and degassing above the surface as well as possible post-eruptive transformations near active fumarolic fields. Lavas from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes display elevated Pb, Zn, Sb, Cu and Ag concentrations in comparison with the average MORB and upper continental crust. Some volcanic samples with both magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing mineral microinclusions have higher Mo, Sn, Sb and Ag contents than previously reported for Kamchatka volcanoes suggesting that sulfur and chlorine potentially contribute to metal enrichment in magmas and associated fluids and facilitate transport of certain ore metals in the Kamchatka subduction zone. Published: 2026-07-04T15:23:16+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.588 (consider)

We have studied the composition and textural setting of these microinclusions along with the petrology and geochemictsry of their host lavas using optical petrography, major and trace element geochemistry and scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS). The microinclusions are represented by Au and Au-bearing alloys, Ag and Ag-bearing alloys, partially oxidized siderophile (W) and chalcophile (Pb, Zn, Cu, Bi, Sb, Pb) metals. item.title: Geochemical, mineralogical, and geochronological constraints on the Wugongshan granite-aplite complex: Implications for regional Li-Nb-Ta mineralization in South China
item.dc:creator: Xiaodong Ma
Bin Wu
Yinqiu Hu
Guanglai Li
Bo Peng
Kai Xu
Yuehu Lyu
item.description: A key obstacle to understanding rare-metal mineralization in highly evolved granitic systems is the uncertain petrogenetic relationship between granites and their felsic derivatives. This study presents an integrated investigation of Mesozoic granites and aplite dikes from the Wugongshan complex in South China, which incorporates whole-rock geochemistry, mineral composition, U-Pb dating of apatite and cassiterite, and apatite Sr isotope. Both rock types crystallized at ∼ 151–150 Ma. Relative to barren granites, the Li-Nb-Ta mineralized aplite are characterized by the crystallization of lepidolite, columbite-(Mn), tantalite-(Mn), and microlite. Geochemical and mineralogical evidence indicates that the aplite crystallized from a more evolved, fluid-rich melts during the magmatic-hydrothermal transition stage. However, mineralogical and Sr isotopic discrepancies between the granites (whole-rock initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7143–0.7208, from literature) and the aplite (apatite initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7512–0.7648) preclude a simple in situ differentiation origin for the aplite from the exposed granites. Instead, it probably originated from a more reduced, isotopically and compositionally heterogeneous underlying batch, either derived from a different deep-seated magma chamber or from the same chamber that sourced the exposed granites but subsequently underwent crustal assimilation at depth. Both alternative models are characterized by high initial 87Sr/86Sr ratios. Regional comparisons with Yichun and Yifeng deposits imply that rare-metal granite-aplite systems in western Jiangxi may result from multiple episodes of granitic magmatism, reflecting heterogeneous source melting and multistage magma recharge on a regional scale, rather than being sole products of continuous fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution. Published: 2026-07-03T23:07:49+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.585 (consider)
